ocr_text: The Joint Declaration : Who will stick up for Hong Kong's right to decide?

U.S. Plans Year-End Drive to Take Iraqi Rebel Areas
American commanders in Iraq, faced with growing insurgency and Jan deadline for national elections, say they are preparing drive to take back Falluja and other rebel-held areas by year's end; say timing will depend on readiness of Iraqi military and police units to occupy city once Americans take it; photos (M)

Sharon Speeds Up Schedule for Gaza Pullout
Prime Min Ariel Sharon announces accelerated timetable for unilateral Israeli withdrawal from Gaza, telling his unhappy Likud party members that plan 'will be implemented, period' ; new schedule would have cabinet decide this month, with parliamentary vote in November; Sharon is concerned about Egyptian commitment to train Palestinian security forces and about new US diplomatic team after US elections (M)

SPORTS BRIEFING: BROADCASTING; A New Sports Radio Network
CSTV and Sirius Satellite Radio announce new college sports network to carry college games in number of sports on radio (S)

PRO FOOTBALL; N.F.C. Scouting Reports
Chart discusses National Football Conference and predicts division winners; photos (M)
N.F.L. Matchup: Week 1

PRO FOOTBALL; N.F.L. MATCHUP: WEEK 1
Matchup between Indianapolis Colts and New England Patriots discussed (S)

Paid Notice: Deaths   SCHARER, JONATHAN M.
SCHARER--Jonathan M. It is with overwhelming sadness that everyone at Overland Entertainment mourns the loss of our beloved founder, fearless leader, mentor, visionary, and most of all, our so dearly loved friend. You will be with us always and are forever in our hearts. We will carry on your legacy and always remember your unparalleled generosity. Our condolences and much love to Vivian and Sy Scharer, Joan Karan, and James Caselli.     SCHARER--Jonathan M. Our deepest condolences to James Caselli, Vivian and Sy Scharer and Joan Karan, the family of our beloved friend Jonathan Scharer. He was the most wonderful, loving, kind friend. We will never forget him. Donna Nadler, Bobby Gould

Sharon's 'Gaza Problem': It May Be Israelis, Not Arabs
Prime Min Ariel Sharon's effort to remove Israeli settlers and military from Gaza and hand it over to Palestinians is running into so many obstacles that even close aides wonder if he will succeed; obstacles include party politics, internal security, economics and international relations; aim of his plan is threefold: to create more easily defensible Israel and leave Gaza to Palestinians; to keep much of West Bank in Israeli hands; to present what he sees as opportunity for Palestinians; moderate Palestinians say they are eager to show they can run Gaza efficiently; prospect of Israeli withdrawal has set off struggle among them for control, both within Yasir Arafat's secular Fatah movement and between Fatah and more radical and Islamic factions, Hamas and Islamic Jihad; photo; map (M)

Checketts and Soros to Invest $25 Million in CSTV
Former Madison Square Garden president Dave Checketts and George Soros's management company will invest $25 million in College Sports Television network, which will feature Mountain West Conference games in 30 sports (S)

Nascar Wasn't Broke, but They Fixed It
Richard Sandomir TV Sports column discusses Nascar's overhaul of race season, which gave fans more incentive to watch races on television and drivers more competition at end of season (M)

Mayor Recommits toAmbitious Recycling Pact
'Bloomberg administration, which largely abandoned recycling two years ago as economic drain, will sign 20-year contract with Hugo Neu Corp, one of nation's largest recycling companies, having concluded that increased landfill prices have made recycling most cost-effective way to get rid of much of New York City's garbage; most ambitious component of plan is construction of $45 million plant on Brooklyn waterfront to recycle all metal, glass and plastic; Hugo Neu will invest $25 million in plant, which is scheduled to be finished by 2007; it will be on city-owned land at South Brooklyn Marine Terminal in Sunset Park; city will spend $20 million to repair pier so it can accommodate barges that will carry roughly 90 percent of material to plant; map (M)

Oil Could Help Japan Resolve Territorial Fight With Russia
Japan's emergence as largest foreign investor in Russia's oil-rich Far East region may help break logjam between two countries over Russia's seizure of Japanese Kurile Islands during World War II; Japanese companies are participating in first $10 billion part of what could be $100 billion development of oil and gas reserves off Russia's Sakhalin Island; Japanese and Russian officials met this summer in Vladivostok to outline plan for 2,500-mile, $12 billion pipeline to bring Siberian oil to Sea of Japan; map; photo (M)

Ex-General Expected to Win Election in Indonesia
If elected, Gen. Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono pledged to continue the civilian rule, curb rampant corruption, create jobs and improve security.

Ex-General Appears to Win by Big Margin in Indonesia
Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono appears to have easily defeated Pres Megawati Sukarnoputri in Indonesia's presidential election; is former general who has promised to uphold civilian rule; would become country's sixth president since independence in 1945; results indicate that Indonesians want change after three years of lackluster leadership from Megawati, who presided over anemic economy and during whose term homegrown radical Islamic group Jemaah Islamiyah carried out three terror attacks; United States did not publicly express its preference in election, but Washington officials made it clear that Yudhoyono has better grasp of how to control Jemaah Islamiyah; photo (M)

C.I.A. Review Is Critical of Prewar Iraq Analysis
Internal document shows that a CIA review has identified serious weaknesses in analytical work on Iraq but continues to hold that prewar conclusion that Iraq possessed illicit weapons was reasonable based on information available at that time; John E McLaughlin, acting director of central intelligence, comments; photo (M)

Russians Crack Down on Security Breaches
Russian lawmakers debate how to respond to terrorist attacks, as Russian investigators arrest airport police supervisor who briefly detained two women suspected of killing themselves and 88 others aboard two passenger airliners last month; in Beslan, three police officers face still-unspecified charges of negligence surrounding seizure of school there that led to deaths of 300 students and adults; arrests, along with dismissal of regional ministers, are first steps by Russian authorities to hold accountable officials whose actions contributed directly or indirectly to wave of terrorist attacks; so far, all those punished have been relatively low-ranking officials (M)

An Upset on Staten Island, in an Upset Republican Party
Fiercely fought Republican primary for Staten Island's state assembly seat shows unrest and strain within party; incumbent Robert A Straniere lost to Vincent Ignizio in surprise upset; Ignizio believes voters considered issues more important than Straniere's longevity; rift in party traced to dispute between former borough president Guy Molinari and Rep Vito J Fossella; photos (M)

The U.S. Has a Favorite in Afghanistan. That's a Problem.
Pres Hamid Karzai, whom US supports, is expected to win Afghanistan's first presidential election; some military commanders and Karzai's challengers say US officials are imperiling election's credibility by trying too hard to get show of broad support for Karzai and doing too little to assure Afghans that electoral playing field is level; photo (M)l

Quality, Not Quantity
Ken Rosenberg letter suggests European golfers are better than US golfers, which is why they won Ryder Cup, in spite of our expectations that US team would prevail (S)

Shockey Must Blame Only Himself
Shockey Must Blame.
Cricket : West Indies' winning revival

Pro-Democracy Forces Falter in Hong Kong
Legislative election campaign in Hong Kong enters final week; series of sexual and financial controversies involving democracy advocates makes it look less and less likely that democrats can win majority; preventing big gains for democrats could vindicate hard-liners in Beijing who have aggressively criticized pro-democracy politicians and have ruled out universal suffrage in Hong Kong for at least next eight years (M)

Rob Lowe Bets on His Wild Side
Alessandra Stanley reviews new CBS series Dr Vegas, starring Rob Lowe; photos (M)

Sun Holds Off Charging Storm and Closes In on W.N.B.A. Title
Connecticut Sun defeats Seattle Storm, 68-64, to lead WNBA finals, 1-0; photo (M)

Tightening Race Increases Stakes of Final Debate
Pres Bush and Sen John Kerry to meet in final presidential debate after two encounters that polls suggest weakened Bush and boosted Kerry; some Republicans who had been confident of victory see debate format favoring Kerry's strengths and are concerned race may be closer than they expected; Bush and Kerry aides comment on candidates' plans for debate and final phase of campaign (M)

BRIDGE; Leaders Emerge in Olympiad
Alan Truscott Bridge column on World Team Olympiad in Istanbul, Turkey; diagrammed deal (M)
Neck and Neck Around the Country

THE BATTLEGROUND; Neck and Neck Around the Country
The presidential campaign has been characterized as a battle in a few closely contested states, but this election also reveals how voters are polarized by region. The coasts are leaning toward John Kerry, while Southern, Plains and Rocky Mountain states are providing President Bush with his firmest support.     The states that are closest are in those regions most in flux. Rapid population growth in the Sunbelt states of Florida and the Southwest has led to bifurcated political cultures in which newcomers are challenging the accepted order. Mr. Kerry is gaining strength in Rust Belt states leaking jobs -- most prominently in Michigan and normally Republican Ohio. And suburbanization in the Upper Midwest is putting generally Democratic states like Minnesota and Wisconsin within Mr. Bush's sights.

With Scraggly Habitat Disappearing, So Is a Rabbit
University of New Hampshire ecology Prof John Litvaitis conducts studies documenting threat to survival of New England cottontail rabbit as its habitat of scraggly land turns into housing developments or forests; photos; US Fish and Wildlife Service moves toward endangered-species designation with broad land-use and economic implications (M)

New iMac Makes Debut a Bit Later Than Apple Hoped
Apple Computer introduces updated version of its iMac home computer with minimalist all-in-one system that hides computer's internal components inside flat-panel LCD display, Apple Expo, Paris; prices for new iMac line will start at $1,299 for 17-inch model; slightly thicker 20-inch model will cost $1,899; photo (M)

U.S. Clears Stent Sales by Guidant
Guidant receives Food and Drug Administration approval to sell stents that are inserted into main neck artery to reduce risk of strokes, making company first manufacturer cleared to sell that version of device in US (M)

Experts Said Slam Would Fail, But a Danish Teenager Made It
Alan Truscott Bridge column on slam played by Danish teen Martin Schaltz; diagram (M)

Floridians Brace for Another Hurricane
Florida braces for possibility of its second major hurricane in less than month; latest predictions suggest landfall for Hurricane Frances north of Palm Beach County; officials in regions along central coast plan evacuations, order schools and courts closed and tell residents of barrier islands to head inland; even people still cleaning up from Hurricane Charley in Orlando and other inland communities are told keep abreast of latest weather reports; photo; map (M)

Protests and Arrests Continue Ahead of Bush's Speech
With everything from basset hounds to banners, demonstrators continued on the final day of the Republican National Convention.

Australia's Wines Expanding Their Global Shelf Space
Australia's McWilliam's Wines plans to increase sales to America substantially; Australia is second-largest source of imported wine in US, having shipped $600 million worth last year, surpassed only by Italy; McWilliam's Wines has new distribution agreement with E & J Gallo Winery; photos (M)
Corrections

A Leader Now Tested by Tragedy
Man in the News profile of Pres George Bush, who, after coming to presidency untested in foreign policy, is now defined by Sept 11 terrorist attacks and two wars while facing electorate deeply divided over whether he rushed into Iraq without adequate cause; described as willful, hands-on manager who accepts guidance from inner circle but follows his own impulse for bold action; his place in history will be contingent upon whether course he has set produces democracy in Iraq; rejects ambiguity for certitude of choice between good and evil and comfort of morality shaped by faith; campaigning style is heavily influenced by Lee Atwater, who conceived attacks for his father's 1988 campaign; acceptance speech at convention opens final acts of political drama of leader seeking ultimate validation of performance; personal style, routine and personality described; career highlights; photos (L)
War and corruption keep Congo tottering

Blue Chips Eke Out Small Gains, but the Nasdaq Falters
Dow Jones industrial average closes up 8.34 points, or 0.08 percent, at 10,047.24; Standard & Poor's 500-stock index ends up 1.75 points, or 0.16 percent, at 1,110.11; Nasdaq composite index finishes down 6.95 points, or 0.37 percent, at 1,879.48; US Treasury prices fall; 10-year note drops 2/32, to 101 26/32; yield rises to 4.03 percent from 4.02 percent; graphs; charts (M)
Minister's revelation surprises Warsaw : Poland sets pullout from Iraq in 2005

Both Parties Pin Hopes on Florida Senate Race
Mel Martinez was the dream candidate for Republican strategists looking for someone to run for the United States Senate from Florida this year.     Born in Cuba, Mr. Martinez fled to Florida at age 15, became a lawyer and eventually was elected chairman of the government of Orange County, home to Orlando and the heart of the state's politically unpredictable but potent midsection. Then in December 2000, President-elect George W. Bush lifted him out of relative obscurity to become secretary of housing and urban development, calling his ''a wonderful story.''
